questions.prompt: A test passed yesterday and fails today with no code change, because the account it reuses had already consumed the single-use coupon. What kind of problem is this?
options: A genuine bug in the coupon feature that should be filed
A data-dependency trap caused by relying on shared, stale state
A boundary-value error in the coupon threshold
A flaky network issue unrelated to the test
questions.explanation: The behaviour under test never changed; the case only failed because it inherited used-up state from a previous run. That is a data-dependency trap, fixed by making the test own fresh data.

questions.prompt: What is the best definition of a precondition for a test case?
options: The first action the tester performs in the test
The expected result the test is checking for
The state that must already be true before step 1 runs
The cleanup steps performed after the test finishes
questions.explanation: A precondition is the assumed setup (e.g. a logged-in user with an empty cart). It is not an action, a result, or teardown.

questions.prompt: You are designing card data for a checkout test sheet. Which set of rows best covers the behaviour?
options: Three different valid cards that all succeed
One valid card, one declined card, and one expired card
Only a declined card, since errors are what break
One valid card reused for every row to keep the sheet simple
questions.explanation: Deliberate data exercises different paths: valid proves the happy path, declined and expired prove distinct error-handling branches. Three valid cards retest the same path.

questions.prompt: Which test is genuinely idempotent and self-contained?
options: It creates the user and coupon it needs, then removes them, so it runs the same way every time
It assumes the test database was seeded correctly by an earlier suite
It works only the first time because it consumes a pre-existing voucher
It requires a tester to manually reset the account before each run
questions.explanation: An idempotent, self-contained test sets up its own data and cleans up after itself, so repeated runs behave identically and nothing leaks between them. The others depend on external or leftover state.
